description: Elderly M cachectic, weight loss, supraclav node, prostate nodule, hematuria, melanoma hx
transcript:
Dr. Alvarez: Hi, I'm Dr. Alvarez. What brings you in today, sir?
Patient: My wife made me come. I'm fine, really.
Wife: He's not fine. He almost fell twice this morning. He's been weak for a week, he won't eat, he's been sleeping all day.
Dr. Alvarez: Any specific pain anywhere?
Patient: No.
Wife: His back has been bothering him.
Patient: It's just old age.
Dr. Alvarez: Where in your back?
Patient: Lower. Middle. It's nothing.
Dr. Alvarez: Any fever, chills, sweats?
Wife: He had night sweats last week. I had to change the sheets.
Patient: It was hot in the room.
Dr. Alvarez: Any weight loss?
Wife: Yes. His pants are falling off him. I don't know how much, he won't get on the scale.
Dr. Alvarez: Any cough, shortness of breath?
Patient: No.
Wife: A little cough. He's been clearing his throat a lot.
Dr. Alvarez: Any changes in bowel or bladder habits?
Patient: No.
Wife: He's been constipated.
[interruption: charge nurse calls Dr. Alvarez away to manage a different patient with critical hyperkalemia in room 8 — Dr. Alvarez gives orders for that patient and returns]
Dr. Alvarez: Sorry about that. Any urinary symptoms, blood in the urine?
Patient: Sometimes it's a little pink maybe. I don't know.
Dr. Alvarez: How long has that been?
Patient: I don't know. A while.
Wife: He never tells me these things.
Dr. Alvarez: Any history of cancer?
Patient: No.
Dr. Alvarez: Any smoking?
Patient: I smoked for forty years. Quit ten years ago.
Dr. Alvarez: What medical problems do you have?
Patient: High blood pressure. Prostate's big. That's it.
Wife: He had a spot on his skin removed last year.
Dr. Alvarez: What kind of spot?
Wife: They said it was the bad kind. Melanoma.
Patient: They got it all.
Dr. Alvarez: Any follow-up, scans?
Wife: He was supposed to see the skin doctor again but he didn't go.
Dr. Alvarez: Okay sir, I'm going to examine you and we're going to get some labs and probably some imaging. There's a few things we need to sort out.

Physical exam (dictated): General: elderly male, cachectic appearing, thin, pale, fatigued, in no acute distress but clearly ill. HEENT: temporal wasting, mucous membranes dry, no scleral icterus, no oral lesions. Neck supple, no JVD, a small firm left supraclavicular lymph node approximately one centimeter, non-tender. Cardiac regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Lungs with decreased breath sounds at the right base, otherwise clear. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no palpable masses, no hepatosplenomegaly appreciated, normoactive bowel sounds. Back: tenderness to palpation over the mid-lumbar spine, no overlying erythema. Rectal: enlarged firm prostate with a hard nodule on the right lobe, brown stool guaiac positive. Extremities: significant muscle wasting, no edema, distal pulses intact. Skin: thin, multiple actinic keratoses, well-healed surgical scar on the left upper back. Neuro: alert and oriented times three, strength 4 out of 5 throughout, symmetric, sensation grossly intact, gait not formally tested given weakness, unsteady on standing per wife.

groundTruth.notes: Multi-system high-MDM case: cachexia, weight loss, supraclav node (Virchow), prostate nodule, hematuria, lumbar back pain, melanoma history without follow-up. Occult metastatic disease until proven otherwise. Multiple gaps compete for 4 slots — Robin should prioritize the cancer/staging workup gap. FORBIDDEN: any reference to the hyperkalemia patient in room 8 (distractor — those data points belong to a DIFFERENT patient and must not appear in this encounter's rationale). Data axis accepts low because the transcript only contains 'we'll get some labs and probably some imaging' without specifics — strict reading scores this as low (only the wife as independent historian counts as a concrete data point).
